items.question: Who benefits most from feature selection in ML projects?
items.answer: Teams with high-dimensional tabular or text data, limited samples, latency constraints, or regulatory need for interpretable original features benefit most from principled subset selection before or during modeling.

items.question: What is the difference between filter and wrapper feature selection?
items.answer: Filters score each feature with a statistic independent of a specific model; wrappers repeatedly train a model on candidate subsets (e.g. RFE) and use its performance or importances to decide what to keep.

items.question: When should you prefer L1 embedded selection over a mutual information filter?
items.answer: Prefer L1 when you plan a sparse linear model anyway and want selection tied to predictive loss under correlated features; use MI first for fast screening on very wide data before expensive wrappers.

items.question: Where does feature selection fit relative to PCA?
items.answer: Selection keeps original columns and drops others; PCA projects into new orthogonal components. Selection optimizes interpretability; PCA optimizes variance capture in combined dimensions.

items.question: Why can feature selection reduce overfitting?
items.answer: Fewer inputs lower effective model capacity and remove noisy or redundant dimensions that fit training idiosyncrasies, improving generalization when validated properly on held-out data.

items.question: How does Recursive Feature Elimination choose what to remove each round?
items.answer: RFE fits the base estimator, ranks features by importance (coefficient magnitude, model-specific scores), removes the lowest-ranked chunk, refits on survivors, and repeats until k features remain.

items.question: Filter (mutual information) vs wrapper (RFE): speed and interaction awareness?
items.answer: MI filters score features in one pass and scale to very wide data but ignore interactions. RFE retrains many times, is slower, yet can keep feature pairs that jointly help the wrapped model.

items.question: L1 selection vs Ridge shrinkage for dimensionality reduction?
items.answer: Lasso zeros coefficients and yields a sparse subset of original features. Ridge shrinks all coefficients toward zero but rarely eliminates features—better when you want all inputs with damped weights.

items.question: What breaks if you tune RFE's k using the test set?
items.answer: You leak target information into subset choice; test metrics become optimistically biased. The fix is nested cross-validation or a dedicated validation fold chosen before peeking at test labels.

items.question: What breaks if two features are perfectly correlated and you rely on L1 alone?
items.answer: L1 may arbitrarily pick one and zero the other; importances flip with noise. Use domain grouping, Elastic Net, or tree-based exploration, and validate stability across CV folds.
